title: Color-Rule-Function Encoding for Combinatorial Memory
abstract: Combinatorial memory is a class of memory in which information is encoded in the set of paths through a structured mesh. In this work, we introduce a systematic encoding framework, referred to as the Color-Rule-Function (CRF) approach, for representing information in combinatorial memory. The method consists of four key steps: selecting a sequence of paths in the mesh, assigning values (e.g., colors) to each cell, defining a set of rules based on the values encountered along each path, and constructing a Boolean function that determines the state of each path. . The coding procedure is illustrated by several examples. The design space scales of the CRF scale fundamentally faster compared to conventional memory. This apparent advantage arises from the use of rule-based and functional representations but is accompanied by increased hardware complexity. A possible hardware realization of the CRF framework is discussed. Importantly, the hardware overhead can be substantially reduced through the use of customized modules. The examples of the customized design are described in the text. The combination of CRF coding with customized module design may lead to a practical advantage in data storage density. According to the estimates, the data storage density may exceed Exabit per centimeter squared. A key problem that requires further investigation is related to the minimum Hamming distance between an arbitrary target bit sequence and the closest sequence realizable within the CRF framework under fixed hardware constraints.
summary: This paper proposes the Color-Rule-Function (CRF) method to encode data into combinatorial memory paths within a structured mesh. It demonstrates that this approach offers a superior scaling ($\mathcal{O}(N^4)$) compared to conventional memories, potentially enabling ultra-high-density storage for read-only applications. The core challenge remains optimizing the rule set and understanding the minimum achievable Hamming distance.
main_problem: To develop a systematic encoding framework for representing information in combinatorial memory that surpasses the scaling limits of conventional storage technologies.
main_result: The Color-Rule-Function (CRF) approach achieves a design space capacity scaling of $\mathcal{O}(N^4)$, potentially leading to data storage densities exceeding $10^{19}$ bits/cm$^2$ in customized hardware realizations.
method: Information is encoded by selecting paths through a structured mesh, assigning values (colors) to cells, defining rules based on path colors, and constructing a final state via a Boolean function.
model_or_system: The system models combinatorial memory using an $N \times N$ mesh structure. The hardware realization involves three modules: a Mesh Module (frequency generators), a Rule Module (frequency decoders/filters), and a Function Module (Boolean logic gates, potentially magnonic).
key_observables: Path states (0 or 1), design space capacity ($	ext{BCRF}$), data storage density (bits/cm$^2$), and the minimum Hamming distance.
important_parameters: $N$ (Mesh dimension), number of colors, number of rules, and the complexity scaling exponents ($N^4$ vs $N^2$).
main_assumptions: The advantage relies heavily on using customized module designs rather than universal ones; finding the optimal CRF encoding is computationally hard.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the general architecture (Mesh $ightarrow$ Rule $ightarrow$ Function), show colored meshes for encoded data examples, and compare scaling trends between different memory types.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the CRF framework via four steps of encoding. It analyzes the theoretical design space scaling ($\mathcal{O}(N^4)$) compared to conventional methods. The discussion then focuses on hardware realization using magnonics and comparing storage density estimates for universal versus customized module designs.
why_interesting: While not directly quantum, the concept of encoding information into complex structural constraints (paths/rules) shares conceptual parallels with topological quantum error correction or state preparation in highly constrained physical systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
